original_abstract:
Members of the humanitarian community have long expressed concern that the Provincial Reconstruction Team (PRT) model in Afghanistan—which integrates military and civil-developmental operations—has created "blurred lines" between combatants and aid workers. Critics argue that this lack of distinction makes non-governmental organizations (NGOs) targets for insurgent violence. While this claim has become a central point of contention in civil-military relations, empirical testing of the link between PRT presence and NGO insecurity has been limited. 

This study evaluates the "blurred lines" hypothesis using a panel-corrected standard error regression analysis covering all 34 Afghan provinces during the high-activity years of 2010 and 2011. While initial findings indicate a general correlation between the presence of PRTs and an increase in security incidents against NGOs, a more nuanced analysis reveals a significant disparity based on the lead nation of the team. The data shows that increased insecurity was specifically associated with provinces containing non-US-led coalition teams, whereas US-led PRTs did not serve as significant predictors of attacks. These results challenge the validity of a universal "blurred lines" theory and suggest that specific operational methodologies, rather than the PRT concept itself, may influence humanitarian safety. The paper concludes by discussing the implications of these findings for future civil-military engagements and suggests that factors such as economic development and local poppy cultivation play significant, often counter-intuitive roles in shaping the security landscape for aid workers.
humanized_abstract:
The influence of PRTs on the humanitarian environment in Afghanistan has been a longstanding area of discussion for the humanitarian community, particularly the notion that their civilian-military approach can lead to “blurred lines” that make it difficult to tell who is who. Insisting that NGOs risk being attacked because of these unclear boundaries, aid leaders and staff often claim that PRTs have contributed to a “security crisis” for humanitarian work. To what extent is this actually true? The connection between PRTs and NGO insecurity is contested, yet it has not been extensively empirically examined in prior research.

Recent academic analysis has made the claim that NGOs who object to the presence of PRTs on the grounds of conflating aid work with the business of the state have their “blurred lines” concerns totally validated (see North and Powers this edition of SWP). Our study which utilised a panel corrected standard error regression (PCSE) approach which captured all security incidents against NGOs in all 34 provinces of Afghanistan for two of the high-activity years of the international intervention – 2010 and 2011 – reveals a first correlation at first glance which holds water for the “blurred lines” theory although it holds in an intriguing fashion: on first inspection our regression revealed that the posting of PRTs was indeed associated with higher incidents. On closer inspection, however, the correlation for the entire data-set holds true for only four provinces when each is inspected in turn – a far cry from total proof or a generally applicable principle or “rule”. A more refined investigation for each province and PRT at time-points (iterations) per annum therefore also reveals that only in the four provinces which exhibited initial positivity do non-US-led (therefore sometimes indirect or proxy-coalition) PRTs, associated in greater frequency with higher incident counts and yet in other instances (six) no coalition PRT is located. Most importantly however has a US-led PRT and these therefore do not constitute a cause for added risk as there were only five provincial incidents for all US-led coalition PRT postings. Thus an absolute validation of the “blurred lines” principle does not exist for the entire sample. What, rather, appears to exist and thus replaces it as a potential factor affecting the safety of aid workers are i) differing operational methodologies from PRT to PRT, and ii) other local variables in different “places”. These have proved on investigations for our data to be considerable. These therefore need to be increasingly factored into humanitarian policy. Factors included here which proved significant – and in the latter category surprisingly for those versed in both counter narcotics cliches and also local geography – include not only provincial poverty level but even local poppy cultivation in terms of being an added source of insecurity to aid-work within some, though by no means all of the provinces included in our data-set.
